title: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman criticizes Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt's January 2020 ban on state-funded travel to California as another in a long line of empty pro-life gestures, arguing that real action requires shutting down abortion facilities and passing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.
audiences: Oklahoma voter sympathetic to Governor Stitt
Pro-life Republican who celebrates symbolic wins

questions.question: What action did Governor Stitt actually take, and how did he frame it?
questions.answer: Stitt announced a ban on state-funded travel by Oklahoma public officials to California, framing it as a tit-for-tat response to California's existing travel ban against Oklahoma and as a defense of Oklahoma's pro-life position.
questions.quote: Governor Kevin Stitt announced on Thursday that there will be no State-funded travel by Oklahoma public officials to California because of California's travel ban against Oklahoma and because of California's pro-choice policies.

questions.question: How did Stitt himself describe his motivation for the travel ban?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voter sympathetic to Governor Stitt
questions.answer: Stitt cast the decision as returning California's gesture, declaring he was proud to govern a state that fights for the unborn as 'the most vulnerable among us.'
questions.quote: If California's elected officials don't want public employees traveling to Oklahoma, I am eager to return the gesture on behalf of Oklahoma's pro-life stance. I am proud to be Governor of a state that fights for the most vulnerable among us, the unborn.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that pro-life gestures, even good ones, are insufficient?
questions.audience: Pro-life Republican who celebrates symbolic wins
questions.answer: Because gestures don't change anything on the ground; they only let politicians appear to be doing good while avoiding substantive action that would actually stop abortions.
questions.quote: But the problem with gestures is that they don't actually change anything. They're great if you're a politician and you want your constituents to think you're doing something good without doing anything of substance or impact.

questions.question: What concrete actions does Silberman say Stitt is morally and constitutionally obligated to take?
questions.answer: Stitt is obligated to abolish abortion outright, shut down the killing centers, and use his executive influence to help pass the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.
questions.quote: Governor Stitt is morally and Constitutionally obligated to abolish abortion. He needs to do that. He needs to shut down the killing centers and use his influence to help pass the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

questions.question: Who does Silberman blame for killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in 2019, and how did the pro-life base enable it?
questions.answer: He blames pro-life Republican Senator Greg Treat, and argues that the celebration of three compromise pro-life bills as victories gave Treat political cover to kill abolition.
questions.quote: The fact that there were still people in Oklahoma who celebrated the three compromise pro-life bills in 2019 is what allowed pro-life Republican Senator Greg Treat to get away with killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

questions.question: What does Silberman say it will take for the killing to actually stop in Oklahoma?
questions.answer: A critical mass of Oklahomans must refuse to be impressed by gestures and compromises and instead demand the full abolition of abortion.
questions.quote: The day when a critical mass of Oklahomans are no longer impressed by gestures and compromises and demand abortion's abolition is the day the killing will stop.

questions.question: Does Silberman think Oklahoma is morally superior to California on abortion?
questions.answer: No. He insists that as long as abortion remains legal in Oklahoma, Oklahomans cannot look down on California because they are equally complicit.
questions.quote: Until then, we can't look down our noses at California. We're just as bad.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Names the Oklahoma pro-life officials Silberman holds responsible for blocking abolition, complementing this article's critique of Stitt and Treat.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Develops the case against Greg Treat, the pro-life senator Silberman cites here as having killed the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Continues the running fight over the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13) that Silberman urges Stitt to champion.

related.title: OK Legislature Sends 3 Useless Pro-Life Bills to Governor Stitt
related.reason: Directly extends the gesture-vs-substance critique by examining later compromise bills sent to Stitt's desk.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Shows the kind of grassroots demand for immediate abolition Silberman says must replace satisfaction with gestures.

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: Concrete case study of a pro-life Oklahoma legislator obstructing abolition, illustrating the pattern Silberman warns about.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Foundational distinction between the abolitionist and pro-life camps that animates Silberman's critique of Stitt's gesture.
